# Product Requirements Document: `sdlc list` command

**Generated by:** requirement-analyst agent / **Date:** 2026-06-12 / **Status:** Draft

## Problem Statement

To answer the simple question "which agents does this project define, and what are their key attributes?", a person today has to open each YAML file under `agents/` by hand and read it field by field. With 6 agents this is already tedious; it does not scale, it is error-prone, and there is no way for a CI script or another tool to obtain the same overview programmatically. There is no single, fast way to get an at-a-glance roster of agents and where each one will be rendered.

## Solution

Add a `list` command to the sdlc-agents CLI. Running `pnpm sdlc list` prints a compact, human-readable table of every agent in the project showing its id, version, phase, model hint, and the output targets it will be rendered to. The same command, with a flag, emits machine-readable output (JSON) so CI pipelines and other tooling can consume the roster without scraping formatted text.

## User Stories

### Story 1 — Human-readable agent overview (Priority: P1)

As a developer working on an sdlc-agents project, I want to run a single command and see a table of all agents with their key fields, so that I can understand the project's agent roster without opening any YAML file.

**Independent test:** In a project containing several agents, run `sdlc list` and confirm the output is a readable table with one row per agent showing id, version, phase, model hint, and targets — verifiable on its own with no other story implemented.

**Acceptance criteria (Gherkin):**
- Given a project with one or more valid agents, When I run `sdlc list`, Then the command prints one row per agent showing id, version, phase, model_hint, and the list of output targets, and exits with code 0.
- Given an agent whose `model_hint` is absent, When I run `sdlc list`, Then that cell renders as a neutral placeholder (e.g. `—`) rather than `undefined` or blank-breaking the table.
- Given a project with zero agents, When I run `sdlc list`, Then the command prints a friendly "no agents found" message and exits with code 0.
- Given the agents are loaded, When the table is printed, Then rows are sorted deterministically (by phase, then id) so output is stable across runs.

### Story 2 — Machine-readable output for CI (Priority: P1)

As a CI script author, I want `sdlc list` to emit structured JSON, so that my pipeline can parse the agent roster programmatically instead of scraping formatted text.

**Independent test:** Run `sdlc list --json` (or `--format json`) and pipe the output into a JSON parser; confirm it parses to an array of agent objects with the expected keys. Works independently of the table rendering.

**Acceptance criteria (Gherkin):**
- Given a project with agents, When I run `sdlc list --json`, Then stdout contains a single valid JSON document (an array of objects) with the keys `id`, `version`, `phase`, `model_hint`, and `targets`, and nothing else (no decorative text) on stdout.
- Given `--json` is passed, When agents have a missing `model_hint`, Then the JSON value for that key is `null` (not omitted, not the string `"—"`).
- Given a project with zero agents, When I run `sdlc list --json`, Then stdout is a valid empty JSON array `[]` and the exit code is 0.

### Story 3 — Clear failure on broken project (Priority: P2)

As a developer, I want `sdlc list` to fail loudly and clearly when the config or an agent file is invalid, so that I trust the roster I see and know exactly what to fix.

**Independent test:** Point the command at a project with a malformed agent YAML or a missing config and confirm it prints a clear error to stderr and exits non-zero.

**Acceptance criteria (Gherkin):**
- Given the project has no resolvable config, When I run `sdlc list`, Then a clear error message is written to stderr and the process exits with a non-zero code.
- Given one agent file fails Zod validation, When I run `sdlc list`, Then the error names the offending agent/file and the command exits non-zero, consistent with how `validate` reports errors.
- Given `--json` is set and loading fails, When the command exits non-zero, Then stdout does NOT contain partial or invalid JSON (errors go to stderr only).

## Functional Requirements

- **FR-001**: The CLI MUST register a new `list` command dispatched from the existing argv parser in the CLI entry point, alongside `init`, `validate`, and `build`.
- **FR-002**: The command MUST load the project configuration via the core `loadConfig(cwd)` interface and load agents via `loadAgents(agentsDir)`, reusing existing core APIs rather than re-reading YAML directly.
- **FR-003**: For each agent the command MUST display: `id`, `version`, `phase`, `model_hint`, and the set of output targets it will be rendered to.
- **FR-004**: The set of output targets per agent MUST be derived from the resolved configuration's `targets` (currently `universal`, `claude-code`, `copilot`), matching what `build` would actually render. [NEEDS CLARIFICATION: are targets global (every agent renders to all configured targets) or can an individual agent restrict its targets via a field not listed in the provided schema?]
- **FR-005**: The default (no flag) output MUST be a human-readable, aligned table on stdout.
- **FR-006**: The command MUST support a machine-readable mode selected by `--json` (and accept `--format json` as an equivalent) producing a single valid JSON array on stdout with one object per agent.
- **FR-007**: In JSON mode, stdout MUST contain only the JSON document — no log lines, headers, or decorative output.
- **FR-008**: A missing `model_hint` MUST render as a placeholder in table mode and as `null` in JSON mode.
- **FR-009**: Agents MUST be emitted in a deterministic order (sort by `phase`, then `id`) in both modes.
- **FR-010**: When zero agents are found, table mode MUST print a friendly message and JSON mode MUST print `[]`; both exit 0.
- **FR-011**: On config load or agent validation failure, the command MUST write a clear, file-attributed error to stderr and exit non-zero, consistent with the existing `validate` command's error behavior.
- **FR-012**: The command MUST be runnable as `pnpm sdlc list` from the repo root and respect the current working directory for config resolution.

## Success Criteria

- **SC-001**: A user can obtain the full agent roster (all five fields for every agent) with a single command invocation and zero manual file opening.
- **SC-002**: The roster overview is produced in under 1 second for a project of up to 50 agents on a typical developer machine.
- **SC-003**: 100% of agents present in the project appear exactly once in the output, with no agent omitted or duplicated.
- **SC-004**: The machine-readable output parses as valid JSON on the first attempt by a standard parser in 100% of runs where the project is valid.
- **SC-005**: When the project is invalid, the command communicates failure (non-zero exit and a stderr message naming the problem) in 100% of such cases, and never emits malformed JSON on stdout.
- **SC-006**: Output ordering is identical across repeated runs of the same project (byte-stable in JSON mode aside from intentional content changes).

## Implementation Decisions

- New command module `list` added to the CLI commands set, following the same shape as the existing `validate` and `build` command modules and wired into the central argv dispatch.
- Reuse core interfaces only: `loadConfig` to obtain `ResolvedConfig` (`targets`, `agentsDir`, `variables`), `loadAgents` to obtain Zod-validated raw agent defs. `resolveAgent` is invoked only if rendered target resolution requires resolved variables; otherwise raw defs suffice for the five display fields.
- A small presentation seam: a pure function that maps the loaded agents + config into a normalized array of row objects (`{id, version, phase, model_hint, targets}`), then two renderers — a table renderer and a JSON serializer — consume that array. This keeps formatting decoupled from loading and makes both output modes test the same normalized data.
- Flag parsing for `--json` / `--format json` handled within the command consistent with how other commands read their options from argv.

## Testing Decisions

- Test at the command seam using vitest, mirroring the existing command tests in the CLI test directory: invoke the command against fixture projects and assert on stdout/stderr/exit behavior.
- Unit-test the normalization function directly (agents+config in, row array out) to cover the deterministic sort, the missing-`model_hint` mapping, and target derivation without rendering.
- Snapshot or structural assertion on the JSON-mode output for a known fixture to lock the schema (`id`, `version`, `phase`, `model_hint`, `targets`).
- Negative-path tests: invalid agent YAML and missing config produce non-zero exit and stderr messages, and JSON mode emits no partial JSON on stdout. Prior art: the existing `validate` command tests already exercise the load/validation failure path and can be used as the reference pattern.
- Empty-project test: zero agents yields the friendly message (table) and `[]` (JSON), exit 0.

## Out of Scope

- Filtering, searching, or querying agents (e.g. `list --phase requirement`) — future enhancement.
- Showing fields beyond the five requested (description, tools_required, inputs, workflow, output_template, policies).
- Per-target preview of the rendered output path or file names.
- Coloured/TTY-adaptive styling, paging, or interactive selection.
- Any write operations or mutation of agent/config files.
- A `--watch` mode.
- Output formats other than the default table and JSON (e.g. YAML, CSV, TSV).

## Assumptions

(Each is a clarifying question I answered with my recommended default and adopted as an explicit assumption.)

- **A-001** — *Which machine-readable format?* Recommended: JSON, since it is the universal CI-friendly default and was the implied intent of "machine-readable." Adopted: JSON only.
- **A-002** — *Flag name for machine-readable mode?* Recommended: support both `--json` (ergonomic) and `--format json` (extensible). Adopted: both, with `--json` as the primary documented form.
- **A-003** — *How is "model hint" rendered when absent?* Recommended: `—` in table mode, `null` in JSON. Adopted as stated in FR-008.
- **A-004** — *Default ordering?* Recommended: deterministic sort by phase then id for stable diffs and predictable CI consumption. Adopted (FR-009).
- **A-005** — *Error-handling style?* Recommended: mirror the existing `validate` command — stderr message naming the file, non-zero exit. Adopted (FR-011).
- **A-006** — *Exit code on empty project?* Recommended: 0 (an empty-but-valid project is not an error). Adopted (FR-010).
- **A-007** — *Source of the targets column?* Recommended: derive from `ResolvedConfig.targets` so the column reflects what `build` would actually emit. Adopted, pending C-1 on whether agents can locally narrow targets.
- **A-008** — *Should JSON include the project's resolved root/agentsDir or be a bare agent array?* Recommended: a bare array of agent objects keeps the contract minimal and matches the story's wording. Adopted; a wrapping metadata object is out of scope.

## Open Clarifications

| # | Question | Status |
|---|----------|--------|
| C-1 | Are output targets global to all agents (every agent renders to every configured target), or can an individual agent declare a subset of targets via a field not present in the provided YAML schema? This determines whether the `targets` column is identical for every row or per-agent. Default assumed: global (all configured targets) per A-007. | Open — non-blocking; assumption adopted |
| C-2 | Should the JSON output be pretty-printed (indented) or compact single-line? Default assumed: pretty-printed for readability, since CI parsers accept both. | Open — non-blocking; assumption adopted |

## Handoff → planner

**Next agent:** planner

**Inputs to provide:**
- This PRD.
- Codebase facts from the context block: CLI dispatch pattern in `packages/cli/src/index.ts`; existing command modules (`init`, `validate`, `build`) as structural references; core APIs `loadConfig`, `loadAgents`, `resolveAgent` and the `ResolvedConfig` shape; the agent YAML schema; the vitest test layout under the CLI package.
- Stories prioritized P1 (Stories 1 and 2 together form the minimum viable increment), then P2 (Story 3).

**Blocking items:**
- None blocking. C-1 (per-agent vs global targets) and C-2 (JSON formatting) are open but each has an adopted default; the planner can proceed on the defaults and revisit only if the resolved-config/agent schema reveals a per-agent targets field.

**Verification note for the planner:** I was unable to read repository files directly in this session (all file-read and shell calls returned no output), so the structural facts above are taken from the provided context block rather than confirmed against source. Before implementation, the planner should verify against the actual code: (a) whether the agent schema or config carries any per-agent target field (resolves C-1), and (b) the exact error-reporting convention used by `validate.ts` so `list` matches it (FR-011).

## Orchestrator resolutions (product-owner proxy, 2026-06-12)

- **C-1 RESOLVED:** targets are **global** — verified against `packages/core/src/schema.ts`: AgentSchema has no per-agent targets field. The `targets` column is identical for every row, sourced from `ResolvedConfig.targets`.
- **C-2 RESOLVED:** **pretty-printed** JSON (2-space indent).
- Note: `model_hint` has a Zod default of `"balanced"`, so after loading it is never absent; FR-008's placeholder path applies only to hypothetical raw data. Planner may simplify accordingly.
